title: `podman image scp` does not use installed SSH binary meaning SSH configuration does not work.
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**



**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Add a new host to `$HOME/.ssh/config`

2. Try `podman image scp` to copy image to new host

**Describe the results you received:**

Host not found.

`Error: failed to connect: dial tcp: lookup newhost: no such host`

**Describe the results you expected:**

Successfully works just like `rsync`, `git`, `sshfs`...

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

It uses `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh` native golang SSH implementation. In contrast `rsync`, `git` and `sshfs` uses the installed binary and communicates over SSH stdin and stdout.

It should be possible to implement an SSH client over external binary which would give a benefit of installed SSH client configuration.

https://github.com/containers/image/issues/958

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.0
API Version:  3.4.0
Go Version:   go1.17.1
Git Commit:   6e8de00bb224f9931d7402648f0177e7357ed079
Built:        Fri Oct  1 13:14:18 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: I could work on this issue. First step would be re implementing `golang.org/x/crypto/ssh` package but over spawned SSH process' stdin and stdout.
I think there is a reason why we use the native golang package. @jwhonce @ashley-cui PTAL

The reason was so that we did not have to rely on random ssh clients on different architectures specifically Macs and Windows. 
Could we try with native and then fail over to execing?

> Could we try with native and then fail over to execing?

I would say it should be the other way. It is much more likely that OpenSSH is already set up on the system and is ready to connect to the remote host.

Sure, as long as we hide this from the user.  
@jwhonce WDYT?

@rhatdan, @baude and I were talking about supporting OpenSSH as well as the current library implementation. Our driver was the go library implementation breaks if the number of keys in ssh-agent is greater than the number of failed attempts supported by sshd. 

We have not gotten as far as an auto-fallback or a flag to denote which method to use.

@jwhonce @rhatdan not sure if I am interpreting this correctly, if we allow for users to manually add and specify hosts, doesn't that kind of open the door for more issues in terms of verifying if a podman socket is active on the other end? Currently `image scp` takes either a connection added and verified via `podman system connection add` as well as new, full urls containing the path to the socket on the remote host. Even the direct connection is kind of iffy and could be messed with if used improperly. The tighter of a lid we keep on this, probably the better? Again, I might be overcomplicating this issue.

@cdoern Show me the command line examples that you would use. Personally I believe that users of this will only(Primarily) expect these commands to work

podman image scp IMAGE user@remotehost:
podman image scp root@remotehost:image .

Anything other then these commands would mean, I got to look at man pages, which would be a bad user experience. Under the covers Podman needs to figure out the best way to handle the command line. Or fail.

@rhatdan these commands both work as long as the `@remotehost` is either one of the configured connections in `podman system connection list` so if `Fedora` was added to the system connections you can do `podman image scp Fedora::alpine` to load the image to your machine. You can also do a full url like `podman image scp alpine charliedoern@192.168.68.126:22/run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock::alpine`

podman image scp Fedora::alpine
Should give a nice error message explaining this.

The other form, no Human being will ever be able to type.  So not really interesting to me.

BTW charliedoern@192.168.68.126:22/run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock::alpine
Podman probably could get a better guess on this, by just connection over ssh and run `printenv XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`.  Is the port number really necessary?

@rhatdan I could switch it so scp assumes port 22, that is a good idea on checking for the env var, I will see if I can implement that. Also, I will add a nicer error message for failed host connection. we have a good warning for when a user connects using a url, informing them that they can use podman system connection add, to specify the host.

I've experimented with combining Golang and OpenSSH.

I think the easiest way would be to share a unix domain socket using `-L` option. It is available since OpenSSH 6.7. (from 2014 so it should be available in all distros)

This is what waypipe does.

`net/rpc` golang package can then used to communicate between processes. (I think you can even use gRPC over unix domain sockets.)

As for socket name I originally thought about using UUID and mount it over /tmp however, there is one of the escape sequence to forward a new socket while connection is open so mounting some socket in XDG_RUNTIME_DIR should be possible.
assigning this to make some small changes in terms of port assumptions and syntax simplicity

Turns out it is quite easy to run RPC over SSH. All I have to do is to use STDIN and STDOUT that are piped over SSH. The client only has to pass the commands that start the matching program to the SSH program.

I have put the library in to this repository.

https://github.com/igo95862/golang-rpc-over-ssh-cmd

I can start experimenting with integrating it with podman next.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@igo95862 @cdoern What is up with this issue?
My SSH RPC library works but I did not have any feedback on it...

@igo95862 I can make some changes for port assumptions if you want. I currently have a PR improving the syntax and error messages for SCP. Other than that I feel like this falls back to a decision from @baude or @jwhonce on supporting openSSH
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Similarly `podman-remote` seems to not respect user SSH config - this would certainly be nice to have.
The SSH config would be on the server side though, correct?

> The SSH config would be on the server side though, correct?

Doesn't the client initiate the connection?
Yes, I misread.  We are using builtin ssh library from golang.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I can *probably* wire this up to look at the ssh config file and make a new podman system connection from that... @containers/podman-maintainers WDYT? This could open some new use cases for scp and for podman remote in general

I don't believe that is the issue.  The problem as I understand it, is that the opessh library we are using for ssh does not fully support the contents of the users ssh config file.  We need to investigate if there is a better library to use, or a way to allow users to exec the ssh command on their platform rather then using the ssh library.

Using SSH binary to connect will open up all features that OpenSSH can use.

For example, OpenSSH supports FIDO2/U2F hardware keys.

I think making it optional makes some sense.  The problem we are trying to avoid is the assumption that all ssh clients are the same, specifically on MAC and Windows. If the ssh executable is optional then it is the users problem if it does not work correctly.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@cdoern don't you have a PR for this?
Yep. This is closed via #15094
